title: Homework_06
text:
library(deSolve)


############################################################
# FUNCTION: run_sir_model
# PURPOSE: Solve deterministic SIR model with deSolve
# INPUTS:
#   beta, gamma : transmission & recovery rates
#   N           : total population
#   S0, I0, R0  : initial conditions
#   t_start     : start time
#   t_end       : end time
#   dt          : timestep
# OUTPUT:
#   data.frame with columns: time, S, I, R
############################################################

run_sir_model <- function(
  beta    = 0.1,
  gamma   = 0.1,
  N       = 1000,
  S0      = 999,
  I0      = 1,
  R0      = 0,
  t_start = 0,
  t_end   = 160,
  dt      = 1
) {

  init  <- c(S = S0, I = I0, R = R0)
  times <- seq(t_start, t_end, by = dt)

  sir_equations <- function(time, state, parameters) {
    with(as.list(c(state, parameters)), {
      dS <- -beta * S * I / N
      dI <-  beta * S * I / N - gamma * I
      dR <-  gamma * I
      list(c(dS, dI, dR))
    })
  }

  out <- ode(
    y     = init,
    times = times,
    func  = sir_equations,
    parms = c(beta = beta, gamma = gamma, N = N)
  )

  as.data.frame(out)
}

sir_model <- run_sir_model()


infected <- function(beta = seq(from = 0, to = 0.5, by = 0.01), gamma = seq(from = 0, to = 0.5, by = 0.01)){
  # row indexing variable
    counter <- 1

  # create storage dataframe
    dfLength <- length(beta)*length(gamma) 
    b_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)
    g_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)
    maxi_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)

  # turn vecs into df
  storageDF <- data.frame(b_out, g_out, maxi_out)

  # enter our loops
  for (i in seq_along(beta)){ # rows
    for (j in seq_along(gamma)){ # cols

      # run sir model
      tmp_df <- run_sir_model(beta = beta[i], gamma = gamma[j])

      # store max I in dataframe
      storageDF$maxi_out[counter] <- max(tmp_df$I)
      storageDF$b_out[counter] <- beta[i] # value for beta stored
      storageDF$g_out[counter] <- gamma[j] # value for gamma stored

      counter <- counter + 1 # increase counter

    }
  }
return(storageDF)
}  
  
max_i <- infected()

Question 3

#two random uniform numbers
set.seed(10)
cord <- c(runif(2)) # generate two random numbers w/ seed set 
cord

[1] 0.5074782 0.3067685

x <- cord[1] # assing first value in cord to x
y <- cord[2] # assign second value in cord to y

#r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2)
#arctan(y/x) = theta

r <- sqrt(x^2 + y^2) # calculate r coordinates and assign to variable r
theta <- atan(y/x) # calculate theta coord and assign to variable theta
polarcor <- c(r, theta) # concatinate r and theta variables and assign to polarcor
print(polarcor)

[1] 0.5929933 0.5437188

title: Homework_03
text:
Question 4

queue <- c("sheep", "fox", "owl", "ant") # make list and name queue

#a - the serpent arrives and gets in line
a <- c(queue, "serpent")
a

[1] "sheep"   "fox"     "owl"     "ant"     "serpent"

#b - the sheep enters the ark
b <- a[-1] # remove the 1st variable and rename
b

[1] "fox"     "owl"     "ant"     "serpent"

#c - the donkey arrives and talks his way to the front of the line
c <- c("donkey", b ) # add a variable to the front and rename
c

[1] "donkey"  "fox"     "owl"     "ant"     "serpent"

#d - the serpent gets impatient and leaves
d <- c[-5] # remove the 5th variable
d

[1] "donkey" "fox"    "owl"    "ant"   

#e - the owl gets bored and leaves
e <- d[-3] # remove the 3rd variable
e

[1] "donkey" "fox"    "ant"   

#f - the aphid arrives and the ant invites him to cut in line
fa <- e[c(1,2)] # make a vector with only the first two variables and rename
fa

[1] "donkey" "fox"   

fb <- e[3] # make a variable with only the ladt variable and rename
fb

[1] "ant"

f <- c(fa, "aphid", fb) # combine the first set, add aphid, combine last set
f

[1] "donkey" "fox"    "aphid"  "ant"   

#g - Finally, determine the position of the aphid in the line
which(f == "aphid") 

[1] 3

# Create a list with the following named elements:
# my_matrix, which is a 4 x 4 matrix filled with random uniform values
# my_logical which is a 100-element vector of TRUE or FALSE values. Do this efficiently by setting up a vector of random values and then applying an inequality to it
# my_letters, which is a 26-element vector of all the lower-case letters in random order.

title: Homework_05
text:
Question #2

# Create a second function that takes the above dataframe as its input and creates a plot of population size over time as a side effect.

# START OF FUNCTION
########################################################################
plot <- function(df = my_df){
    # FUNCTION: plot()
    # PURPOSE: Make a scatter plot of my_df a logistic growth model
    # INPUTS: my_df: t = time, n = population size
    # OUTPUTS: scatterplot of Population Size over Time
df %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = t,
             y = n)) + #define axis
  geom_point(size = 1) + #scatter plot, and size of point
  labs(
    x = "Time (year)",
    y = "Population Size") + #rename the axis
  theme_classic() + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,210), expand = c(0.02,0.02)) + #fix scale on both axis
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,100), expand = c(0.02,0.02))
  
}
########################################################################
# END OF FUNCTION

plot()

title: Homework_06
text:
library(deSolve)


############################################################
# FUNCTION: run_sir_model
# PURPOSE: Solve deterministic SIR model with deSolve
# INPUTS:
#   beta, gamma : transmission & recovery rates
#   N           : total population
#   S0, I0, R0  : initial conditions
#   t_start     : start time
#   t_end       : end time
#   dt          : timestep
# OUTPUT:
#   data.frame with columns: time, S, I, R
############################################################

run_sir_model <- function(
  beta    = 0.1,
  gamma   = 0.1,
  N       = 1000,
  S0      = 999,
  I0      = 1,
  R0      = 0,
  t_start = 0,
  t_end   = 160,
  dt      = 1
) {

  init  <- c(S = S0, I = I0, R = R0)
  times <- seq(t_start, t_end, by = dt)

  sir_equations <- function(time, state, parameters) {
    with(as.list(c(state, parameters)), {
      dS <- -beta * S * I / N
      dI <-  beta * S * I / N - gamma * I
      dR <-  gamma * I
      list(c(dS, dI, dR))
    })
  }

  out <- ode(
    y     = init,
    times = times,
    func  = sir_equations,
    parms = c(beta = beta, gamma = gamma, N = N)
  )

  as.data.frame(out)
}

sir_model <- run_sir_model()


infected <- function(beta = seq(from = 0, to = 0.5, by = 0.01), gamma = seq(from = 0, to = 0.5, by = 0.01)){
  # row indexing variable
    counter <- 1

  # create storage dataframe
    dfLength <- length(beta)*length(gamma) 
    b_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)
    g_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)
    maxi_out <- rep(NA, dfLength)

  # turn vecs into df
  storageDF <- data.frame(b_out, g_out, maxi_out)

  # enter our loops
  for (i in seq_along(beta)){ # rows
    for (j in seq_along(gamma)){ # cols

      # run sir model
      tmp_df <- run_sir_model(beta = beta[i], gamma = gamma[j])

      # store max I in dataframe
      storageDF$maxi_out[counter] <- max(tmp_df$I)
      storageDF$b_out[counter] <- beta[i] # value for beta stored
      storageDF$g_out[counter] <- gamma[j] # value for gamma stored

      counter <- counter + 1 # increase counter

    }
  }
return(storageDF)
}  
  
max_i <- infected()

title: Homework_06
text:
Question 2

library(ggplot2)
library(magrittr)


heat_map <- function(data = max_i){

  plot <- max_i %>% 
    ggplot(aes(x = b_out,
             y = g_out,
            fill = maxi_out)) + #define axis
    geom_tile()+ #scatter plot, and size of point
    labs(
        x = "Beta",
        y = "Gamma") + #rename the axis
    theme_classic()
  
  return(plot)
}

heat_map()

title: Homework_07
text:
Question 8

irisFinal <- iris %>%
  filter(Species=="virginica" | Species=="versicolor", Sepal.Length > 6 & Sepal.Width > 2.5) %>% #Q2 iris2
  select(Species, Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width) %>% #Q3 iris3
  mutate(Sepal_Area= Sepal.Length*Sepal.Width) %>% #Q4 iris4
  group_by(Species) %>%
  summarize(Average_Sepal_Length=mean(Sepal.Length, totalnumber = n()),
            Average_Sepal_Width=mean(Sepal.Width, totalnumber = n()),
          Sample.Size = n()) #Q6 iris6

title: Homework_09
text:
Question 1

# Continouus X = Petal Length
# Continuous Y = Petal Width

q1 <- iris %>% ggplot(aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Petal.Width)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    x = "Petal Length (cm)",
    y = "Petal Width (cm)") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,3), expand = c(0.02,0.02)) + #fix scale on both axis
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,8), expand = c(0.02,0.02))

q1

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Homework_09
text:
Question 2

# Discrete X = Species
# Continuous Y = Petal Width

q2 <- iris %>% ggplot(aes(x = Species, y = Petal.Length)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "pink") +
  geom_jitter() + 
  labs(
    x = "Species Type",
    y = "Petal Length (cm)") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,8), expand = c(0.02,0.02))

q2

title: Homework_09
text:
Question 3

library(tidyverse)

# Continuous X = Petal Length
# Discrete Y = Is setosa (binary)

df <- iris %>% mutate(is_setosa = case_when(
  Species == "setosa" ~ 1,
  Species %in% c("virginica", "versicolor") ~ 0,))



q3 <- df %>% ggplot(aes(x = Petal.Length, y = is_setosa)) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "glm", method.args = list(family = "binomial")) +
  labs(
    x = "Petal Length (cm)",
    y = "Is Setosa Species") +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0,1.5), expand = c(0.02,0.02)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,8), expand = c(0.02,0.02))

q3

text:
Question 2

md = {
    "first":"Hannah",
    "last":"McNulty",
    "year":2026,
    "status":"active",
    "mood":"happy"
}

keys = md.keys()
print(keys)

values = md.values()
print(values)

md["favcolor"] = "green" # Adds an element to the bottom of the existing dictionary.
print(md)

md.items() # Prints all pairs in a list (tuples)

md["study"] = "plants" # add a new element
print(md)

del md["year"] # delete an element
print(md)

dict_keys(['first', 'last', 'year', 'status', 'mood'])
dict_values(['Hannah', 'McNulty', 2026, 'active', 'happy'])

title: Homework_12
text:
import json
import pandas as pd 
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go 

# data download
gap = px.data.gapminder().query("year==2007")

# filter
europe = gap.query("continent == 'Europe'")



fig = px.choropleth( 
    europe,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color = "gdpPercap",
    hover_name = "country",
    hover_data = {
        "gdpPercap": ":,.0f",
        "pop": ":,",
        "iso_alpha" : False
    },
    color_continuous_scale = "Viridis", 
    title = "Europe 2007 - GDP per capita and Population by country"
)
fig.show()

# smaller map to only show europe
fig.update_geos( 
    scope = "europe",
    showland = True,
    landcolor = "rgb(240,240,240)"

)
fig.show()


# add projection - Natural Earth

fig2 = px.choropleth( 
    europe,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color = "gdpPercap",
    hover_name = "country",
    hover_data = {
        "gdpPercap": ":,.0f",
        "pop": ":,",
        "iso_alpha" : False
    },
    color_continuous_scale = "Viridis", 
    projection = "natural earth",
    title = "Europe 2007 - GDP per capita and Population by country"
)

fig2.update_geos(
    scope = "europe",
    showland = True,
    landcolor = "rgb(240,240,240)"
    
)
fig2.show()

# I added the natural earth projection using the projection
#  = "natural earth" argument. This projection allows for 
# the countries to be larger and occupy a much more 
# familiar shape than the default projection that was applied.

title: Homework_12
text:
import json
import pandas as pd 
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go 

# data download
gap = px.data.gapminder().query("year==2007")

# filter
europe = gap.query("continent == 'Europe'")



fig = px.choropleth( 
    europe,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color = "gdpPercap",
    hover_name = "country",
    hover_data = {
        "gdpPercap": ":,.0f",
        "pop": ":,",
        "iso_alpha" : False
    },
    color_continuous_scale = "Viridis", 
    title = "Europe 2007 - GDP per capita and Population by country"
)
fig.show()

# smaller map to only show europe
fig.update_geos( 
    scope = "europe",
    showland = True,
    landcolor = "rgb(240,240,240)"

)
fig.show()


# add projection - Natural Earth

fig2 = px.choropleth( 
    europe,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color = "gdpPercap",
    hover_name = "country",
    hover_data = {
        "gdpPercap": ":,.0f",
        "pop": ":,",
        "iso_alpha" : False
    },
    color_continuous_scale = "Viridis", 
    projection = "natural earth",
    title = "Europe 2007 - GDP per capita and Population by country"
)

fig2.update_geos(
    scope = "europe",
    showland = True,
    landcolor = "rgb(240,240,240)"
    
)
fig2.show()

# I added the natural earth projection using the projection
#  = "natural earth" argument. This projection allows for 
# the countries to be larger and occupy a much more 
# familiar shape than the default projection that was applied.

title: Homework_12
text:
Question 2

fig3 = px.scatter_geo(
    gap,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color="continent",
    size = 'pop',
    hover_name="country",
    hover_data = {
        "pop": ":,",
        "lifeExp":":1f",
        "iso_alpha" : False,
        "continent": False
    },
    title = "Country Population by Continent"
)
fig3.show()



# add labels

fig4 = px.scatter_geo(
    gap,
    locations = "iso_alpha",
    color="continent",
    size = 'pop',
    hover_name="country",
    hover_data = {
        "pop": ":,",
        "lifeExp":":1f",
        "iso_alpha" : False,
        "continent": False
    },
    title = "Country Population by Continent",
    labels = {
        "lifeExp": "Life Expectancy",
        "pop": "Population"}
)
fig4.show()

# I adjusted the labels using the labels argument.
#  With this argument I changed lifeExp to Life 
# Expectancy and pop to Population for readability 
# and comprehension.

text: Question 4

title: Homework_08
text:
A

library(ggplot2)
set.seed(27)


trt_group <- c(rep("Hannah",5), rep("Bryan",6), rep("Jackson",4)) # define groups

z <- c(runif(4) + 1, runif(5) + 10, runif(6)+ 27) # define responses

df <- data.frame(trt=trt_group,res=z) # make a dataframe

obs <- tapply(df$res,df$trt,mean)
obs

    Bryan    Hannah   Jackson 
15.903616  3.296177 27.741784 

# create a simulated data set
df_sim <- df # set up a new data frame

rando <- df_sim$res <- sample(df_sim$res) # randomize assignment of response to treatment groups
print(rando)

 [1] 27.616095 27.738323 10.002450  1.971750 27.849618 27.502747  1.873870
 [8] 27.876448 10.137094 27.191909 10.401648 10.222276  1.083758 10.072499
[15]  1.329231

title: Homework_08
Homework_08
text:
C

n_reps   <- 100
results  <- data.frame(replicate = 1:n_reps, Bryan     = NA_real_, Hannah    = NA_real_, Jackson   = NA_real_)

for (i in 1:n_reps) {
  means          <- shuffle_data(df_sim)   
  results$Bryan[i]   <- means["Bryan"]
  results$Hannah[i]  <- means["Hannah"]
  results$Jackson[i] <- means["Jackson"]
}

head(results)

4         4 13.08225 20.61150 10.329681

title: Homework 11: Structured Programming and CA Models”
text:
This repository contains this .qmd file, which has the github repository link and driver. Also, it contains a functions folder with draw.py, init.py, mouse.py, and update.py script files to run the model.
https://github.com/hmcnulty/CA_SIR_6100

title: Homework 11: Structured Programming and CA Models”
text:
This repository contains this .qmd file, which has the github repository link and driver. Also, it contains a functions folder with draw.py, init.py, mouse.py, and update.py script files to run the model.
https://github.com/hmcnulty/CA_SIR_6100
